refined_fr.refined_fr: Maintain teleoperation link availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold throughout the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR 'achieve the customer-defined teleoperation link availability' is vague about the temporal scope and measurement context. The refined version clarifies that availability is a sustained property (not a one-time achievement) measured over a defined period, and that the threshold is customer-specified rather than system-imposed. This remains solution-neutral: it does not prescribe redundancy, network architecture, or failover mechanisms—only the outcome requirement. The FR is atomic: it expresses a single obligation (maintain availability above threshold) without embedded 'and'/'or' conditions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect teleoperation link uptime logs over the agreed evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days). Calculate availability as (total_time - downtime) / total_time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., 0.999). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ target.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant communication pathways with automatic failover logic to maintain continuous link availability when primary pathway degrades or fails.
DP2: Deploy real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection and proactive rerouting to alternative network resources before availability threshold is breached.
DP3: Establish tiered network architecture (primary, secondary, tertiary) with load balancing and dynamic path selection based on measured latency, packet loss, and jitter metrics.
DP4: Implement adaptive codec and compression strategies that degrade gracefully under poor link conditions, maintaining minimum viable teleoperation capability above availability threshold.
DP5: Design link availability assurance through contractual SLA enforcement with carrier/ISP partners, backed by financial penalties and guaranteed restoration time commitments.
DP6: Combine redundant pathways (DP1) with real-time monitoring (DP2) and adaptive degradation (DP4) to create a multi-layer availability strategy with no single point of failure.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP6: Combine redundant communication pathways with automatic failover, real-time link quality monitoring with predictive rerouting, and adaptive payload degradation to maintain availability above threshold through defense-in-depth across network, detection, and graceful degradation layers.

potential_sources: Redundant pathways (DP1 component) and adaptive degradation (DP4 component) must coordinate on minimum viable bandwidth thresholds—risk of conflicting decisions if not unified under single availability policy.
Monitoring logic (DP2 component) and failover logic (DP1 component) share state (link quality metrics)—requires clear interface contract to avoid race conditions or stale data.
Load balancing across pathways and adaptive codec selection both consume network capacity—requires coordinated resource allocation to prevent oscillation or thrashing.
coupling_check.notes: DP6 is logically sound but introduces coordination complexity across three sub-layers. Coupling is manageable if: (1) a unified 'Link Availability Controller' owns the decision logic and state, (2) each sub-layer (redundancy, monitoring, adaptation) exposes clean interfaces (e.g., 'current_link_quality', 'failover_triggered', 'codec_bitrate_adjusted'), and (3) a priority hierarchy is defined (e.g., failover takes precedence over codec adjustment). Recommend explicit architectural pattern (e.g., state machine or event-driven orchestration) to manage coupling.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Multiple independent communication pathways (e.g., cellular, satellite, fixed-line) are available at the worksite. RISK: If only one pathway exists, DP1 (redundancy) cannot be realized; fallback to DP2+DP4 only.

ASSUMPTION: Link quality metrics (latency, packet loss, jitter) can be measured in real-time with acceptable overhead. RISK: Measurement itself consumes bandwidth; if overhead exceeds 5% of available capacity, monitoring may degrade the link it is meant to protect.

ASSUMPTION: Adaptive codec/compression can degrade gracefully without rendering teleoperation unsafe or unusable. RISK: If safety-critical functions require high-fidelity video/data, aggressive degradation may violate worksite safety plan; requires explicit safety analysis.

ASSUMPTION: Failover and rerouting can complete within acceptable latency bounds (e.g., <500ms) to maintain operator situational awareness. RISK: If failover latency exceeds operator reaction time, availability metric may be met but safety may be compromised.

ASSUMPTION: Carrier/ISP SLA enforcement (DP5 alternative) is not sufficient alone; system must be self-healing. RISK: Relying solely on contractual penalties does not guarantee real-time availability during outages.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough (weeks/months) to capture rare failure modes and seasonal network variations. RISK: Short evaluation periods may not expose edge cases; 0.999 availability requires ~43 minutes of downtime per month—rare events may not manifest.

ASSUMPTION: Operator training and worksite policies support graceful degradation and failover events. RISK: If operators are not trained to recognize and respond to link quality changes, system availability may be technically met but operationally unsafe.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly separate availability (link is up) from usability (link is up AND sufficient quality for safe teleoperation). Propose revised FR: 'Maintain teleoperation link availability at ≥0.999 AND link quality (latency <X ms, packet loss <Y%, jitter <Z ms) sufficient for safe operation per worksite safety plan throughout the evaluation period.' This clarifies the boundary between DP1 (redundancy for availability) and DP4 (adaptation for usability) and ensures safety is not traded for availability metrics. Additionally, recommend explicit definition of 'evaluation period' (duration, seasonal coverage, failure injection testing) to validate that 0.999 target is achievable and meaningful in the operational context.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for managing all communication pathways between operator and vehicle, including redundancy coordination, link quality monitoring, failover logic, and adaptive payload degradation policies.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface to physical/logical network layer; Teleoperation module monitors link quality metrics and issues failover commands; receives real-time link status and capacity constraints.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and mission parameters; Teleoperation adapts payload encoding/degradation based on available bandwidth and link quality.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state and sensor data to operator; payload degradation logic selectively reduces telemetry fidelity under poor link conditions while maintaining safety-critical data.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety thresholds and alerts; ensures safety-critical messages bypass degradation and use all redundant pathways; communicates availability status to Safety Services.

module_containment_scores.notes: Strong containment: Teleoperation is the natural locus for communication pathway management, failover orchestration, and adaptive payload handling. Redundancy coordination, link monitoring, and graceful degradation are core teleoperation concerns. Minor diffusion: Network Link layer must implement physical failover mechanics; Safety Services must enforce availability thresholds and safety-critical message prioritization.

Poor containment: Vehicle module owns vehicle-side hardware and actuation; communication pathway redundancy and operator-side failover logic belong in Teleoperation. Significant diffusion: Redundancy, link monitoring, and payload degradation would scatter across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Network Link, violating cohesion.

Weak containment: Safety Services enforces safety policies and thresholds but does not own communication infrastructure or failover mechanics. Payload degradation and link monitoring are operational concerns, not safety policy. Significant diffusion: Core DP logic (redundancy, failover, adaptive degradation) would remain in Teleoperation; Safety Services would only validate availability thresholds.

Very poor containment: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not an implementation module. Placing DP6 here would diffuse responsibility across all child modules without clear ownership. Violates principle of specific module boundaries.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP6 is fundamentally a teleoperation communication resilience strategy. The Teleoperation module owns the operator-vehicle communication channel, link quality monitoring, failover decision logic, and payload adaptation policies. By centralizing these concerns in Teleoperation, we achieve high cohesion (all redundancy, monitoring, and degradation logic co-located) and minimize diffusion (Network Link handles physical failover mechanics; Safety Services validates availability thresholds; Vehicle receives adapted payloads). This boundary aligns with the principle that communication resilience is a teleoperation concern, not a vehicle or safety policy concern.
containment_risks: Risk: Network Link layer must implement physical redundancy and failover mechanics (e.g., switching between cellular, satellite, mesh pathways). Teleoperation module cannot fully own network infrastructure; coordination interface must be clear and well-defined.

Risk: Safety Services must enforce availability thresholds and ensure safety-critical messages are never degraded. Teleoperation must respect SafetyAlert interface constraints; if thresholds are violated, Teleoperation must escalate to Safety Services rather than degrade safety-critical payloads.

Risk: Vehicle module must handle incoming degraded payloads gracefully (e.g., reduced sensor fidelity, lower-resolution video). Teleoperation must document payload degradation contracts; Vehicle must validate and handle all degradation modes.

Risk: Predictive rerouting logic may require machine learning or statistical models. If model training/updates are frequent, ownership of model lifecycle (training, validation, deployment) must be clarified between Teleoperation and external data/ML services.
Risk: Real-time link quality monitoring may require low-latency telemetry from Network Link. Latency of monitoring feedback could impact failover responsiveness; interface SLAs must be explicit.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FEASIBLE with explicit SLA definitions. Acceptance criterion (link availability ≥ 0.999 over evaluation period) is measurable and traceable to Teleoperation module's failover and adaptive degradation logic. Verification requires: (1) instrumentation of link quality metrics across all redundant pathways; (2) automated failover event logging with timestamps; (3) payload degradation event tracking; (4) end-to-end availability calculation across evaluation period. Safety-critical message bypass must be auditable to ensure safety thresholds are never violated during degradation.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. Teleoperation module is the natural owner of operator-vehicle communication resilience. FR allocation is unambiguous: Teleoperation must maintain availability ≥ 0.999 through DP6 mechanisms (redundancy, monitoring, failover, adaptive degradation). Allocation to Teleoperation is justified by high containment score (0.85) and clear ownership of communication channel, link monitoring, and failover orchestration. External dependencies (Network Link physical failover, Safety Services threshold enforcement, Vehicle payload handling) are well-scoped as interface contracts rather than ownership diffusion.

diffusion_risks: NETWORK LINK INTERFACE: Physical redundancy mechanics (cellular/satellite/mesh switching) reside in Network Link layer, not Teleoperation. Failover command interface must define: (1) command latency SLA (e.g., ≤100ms decision-to-switch); (2) link status reporting frequency (e.g., ≥10Hz for real-time monitoring); (3) atomic failover guarantees (no message loss during switch). Lack of explicit SLA creates risk of failover delays exceeding availability target.

SAFETY SERVICES ESCALATION: Teleoperation must not unilaterally degrade safety-critical payloads. SafetyAlert interface must enforce: (1) pre-defined safety-critical message list (e.g., emergency stop, collision alerts); (2) escalation protocol when link quality threatens safety-critical delivery; (3) Safety Services veto authority over degradation policies. Risk: If Teleoperation degrades safety-critical data without Safety Services approval, availability gains are negated by safety violations.

VEHICLE PAYLOAD DEGRADATION CONTRACTS: Teleoperation defines degradation modes (e.g., video resolution reduction, sensor sampling rate reduction, command latency tolerance). Vehicle module must validate all degradation modes and handle gracefully. Risk: If Vehicle cannot handle a degradation mode (e.g., crashes on reduced-fidelity sensor input), Teleoperation's adaptive strategy fails. Explicit payload degradation contract (with version control and validation matrix) is required.

PREDICTIVE REROUTING MODEL LIFECYCLE: DP6 includes 'predictive rerouting' logic, implying machine learning or statistical models. Ownership of model training, validation, deployment, and updates must be clarified. Risk: If model ownership is ambiguous, model staleness or validation gaps could degrade failover effectiveness. Recommend explicit IM boundary for 'Link Quality Prediction Service' with clear SLA for model freshness and accuracy.

REAL-TIME LINK MONITORING LATENCY: Link quality monitoring feedback from Network Link to Teleoperation must be low-latency to enable responsive failover. Risk: If monitoring latency exceeds failover decision time (e.g., 500ms monitoring delay + 100ms decision + 100ms switch = 700ms total), availability target may be missed during transient link degradation. Explicit latency SLA (e.g., ≤50ms end-to-end monitoring loop) is required.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: STRONG CONTAINMENT (0.85 score justified). Teleoperation module correctly owns: (1) redundant pathway coordination (deciding which pathways are active); (2) link quality monitoring logic (collecting and analyzing metrics); (3) failover decision engine (when and how to switch); (4) adaptive payload degradation policies (what to degrade and when). MINOR DIFFUSION (0.15 score) is appropriate and well-scoped: Network Link owns physical failover mechanics (hardware switching); Safety Services owns safety threshold enforcement (policy validation); Vehicle owns payload handling (graceful degradation implementation). These are interface responsibilities, not ownership diffusion. Teleoperation remains the single source of truth for communication resilience strategy.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP6 directly and sufficiently addresses FR. FR requires 'maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 throughout evaluation period.' DP6 provides three complementary mechanisms: (1) redundant communication pathways with automatic failover (increases availability by eliminating single points of failure); (2) real-time link quality monitoring with predictive rerouting (enables proactive failover before link degradation causes unavailability); (3) adaptive payload degradation (maintains availability by gracefully reducing fidelity rather than losing connection). Together, these mechanisms form a defense-in-depth strategy that directly maps to the availability target. DP6 is complete, non-redundant, and implementable. No gaps identified between FR and DP.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1 - NETWORK LINK SLA: Add explicit interface SLA to Network Link boundary: 'Failover command execution latency ≤100ms; link status reporting frequency ≥10Hz; atomic failover with zero message loss guarantee.' Document failover mechanics (e.g., which pathways are redundant, switching logic, timeout thresholds).

REVISION 2 - SAFETY CRITICAL MESSAGE BYPASS: Formalize SafetyAlert interface contract: (1) define safety-critical message list (e.g., emergency stop, collision alerts, loss-of-signal recovery); (2) require Teleoperation to route safety-critical messages through all redundant pathways without degradation; (3) require Safety Services to validate availability thresholds and veto degradation policies that violate safety constraints; (4) add escalation protocol: if link quality threatens safety-critical delivery, Teleoperation must alert Safety Services and await approval before degrading.

REVISION 3 - PAYLOAD DEGRADATION CONTRACT: Create explicit 'Payload Degradation Matrix' document owned by Teleoperation, specifying: (1) all supported degradation modes (e.g., video resolution levels, sensor sampling rates, command latency tolerances); (2) conditions triggering each mode (e.g., link quality thresholds); (3) Vehicle module validation checklist (e.g., 'Vehicle must handle 480p video without crashing'). Require Vehicle module to sign off on all degradation modes before deployment.

REVISION 4 - LINK QUALITY PREDICTION SERVICE: If predictive rerouting uses machine learning, create explicit IM boundary 'Link Quality Prediction Service' with: (1) model training/validation/deployment SLA (e.g., model updated weekly, validated on holdout test set); (2) prediction accuracy target (e.g., ≥95% precision for link degradation detection); (3) fallback strategy if model is unavailable (e.g., revert to reactive failover). Clarify ownership: Teleoperation consumes predictions; external ML service owns model lifecycle.

REVISION 5 - MONITORING LATENCY SLA: Add explicit latency requirement to Network Link interface: 'Link quality monitoring feedback latency ≤50ms end-to-end (collection + transmission + processing).' Justify latency target based on failover decision time and availability target (e.g., if failover takes 100ms and link degradation lasts 200ms, monitoring latency must be <100ms to catch degradation before availability is lost).

REVISION 6 - AVAILABILITY MEASUREMENT PROTOCOL: Define measurement protocol for acceptance criterion (≥0.999 availability): (1) measurement window (e.g., 30-day evaluation period); (2) availability calculation method (e.g., (total_time - downtime) / total_time); (3) what counts as 'downtime' (e.g., any period where link quality falls below minimum threshold for >1 second); (4) instrumentation requirements (e.g., Teleoperation must log all failover events, link quality samples, degradation events with timestamps); (5) audit trail for safety-critical message delivery (e.g., log all safety-critical messages and confirm delivery without degradation).

REVISION 7 - GRACEFUL DEGRADATION PRIORITY MATRIX: Formalize priority order for adaptive degradation when link quality degrades: (1) safety-critical messages (never degrade); (2) operator commands (minimal degradation); (3) vehicle telemetry (moderate degradation allowed); (4) sensor data (aggressive degradation allowed). Require Safety Services to approve priority matrix before deployment.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, DP-IM, FR-DP) pass with strong technical justification, confirming that FR6 is well-formed and DP6 is sound. However, the gate analysis identifies seven critical interface contracts and safety-critical design decisions that must be formalized BEFORE decomposition proceeds. The high safety criticality of teleoperation (operator commands, emergency stop, collision alerts) combined with adaptive degradation logic creates cascading risks if interface SLAs and safety bypass rules are not explicit. Revising DP6 to incorporate these interface contracts as formal design specifications will eliminate ambiguity in downstream decomposition and prevent implementation-phase rework. This is a REVISE_CURRENT_LEVEL decision, not STOP, because the FR and DP are fundamentally sound; the revision strengthens their realization without changing the core logic.

revise.why: DP6 currently states three mechanisms (redundant pathways, link monitoring, adaptive degradation) at a logical level. The gate analysis reveals that successful realization depends on seven explicit interface contracts and safety policies that are not yet part of the DP specification. Revising DP6 to include these contracts as formal design specifications will: (1) eliminate diffusion risks by clarifying ownership boundaries (Teleoperation vs. Network Link vs. Safety Services vs. Vehicle); (2) make verification feasible by defining measurable SLAs for failover latency, monitoring latency, and safety-critical message handling; (3) reduce safety risk by formalizing the safety-critical message bypass protocol and escalation rules; (4) enable downstream decomposition to inherit clear interface requirements rather than discovering them during implementation. The revision should structure DP6 as: DP6.1 (Redundant Pathway Coordination with Network Link SLA), DP6.2 (Real-Time Link Monitoring with Latency SLA), DP6.3 (Predictive Rerouting with Model Lifecycle SLA), DP6.4 (Adaptive Payload Degradation with Safety-Critical Bypass Protocol), DP6.5 (Availability Measurement and Audit Protocol). This decomposition of DP6 into sub-DPs will naturally flow into sub-FRs once the interface contracts are formalized.

assumptions: Assumption A1: Network Link module can execute failover commands with latency ≤100ms and report link status at ≥10Hz. (CRITICAL: Must be validated with Network Link owner before proceeding.)

Assumption A2: Safety Services module has authority to veto Teleoperation's degradation policies and can enforce safety-critical message delivery without degradation. (CRITICAL: Must be formalized in Safety Services interface contract.)

Assumption A3: Vehicle module can gracefully handle all degradation modes specified in Payload Degradation Matrix without crashes or safety violations. (CRITICAL: Must be validated with Vehicle owner and documented in Payload Degradation Contract.)

Assumption A4: Link quality prediction model (if ML-based) can achieve ≥95% precision for link degradation detection and can be updated weekly without disrupting failover logic. (CRITICAL: Must be clarified with ML service owner.)

Assumption A5: Teleoperation module can instrument and log all failover events, link quality samples, degradation events, and safety-critical message delivery with ≤50ms latency. (CRITICAL: Must be validated with instrumentation/observability team.)

Assumption A6: Evaluation period for availability measurement is ≥30 days and captures representative link degradation scenarios (transient failures, sustained outages, predictable patterns). (CRITICAL: Must be agreed with stakeholders.)
Assumption A7: Safety-critical message list is stable and known at design time (e.g., emergency stop, collision alerts, loss-of-signal recovery). (CRITICAL: Must be formalized by Safety Services.)
open_risks: RISK R1 (FAILOVER LATENCY): If Network Link failover execution latency exceeds 100ms, or if link status reporting frequency drops below 10Hz, Teleoperation may not detect link degradation in time to prevent availability loss. Mitigation: Formalize Network Link SLA with explicit latency and frequency targets; conduct latency stress tests under worst-case conditions (e.g., high CPU load, network congestion).

RISK R2 (SAFETY-CRITICAL BYPASS AMBIGUITY): If safety-critical message list is not formalized, or if Teleoperation degrades safety-critical messages without Safety Services approval, availability gains are negated by safety violations. Mitigation: Formalize safety-critical message list with Safety Services; implement escalation protocol requiring Safety Services veto authority; add audit trail for all safety-critical message handling.

RISK R3 (PAYLOAD DEGRADATION INCOMPATIBILITY): If Vehicle module cannot handle a degradation mode (e.g., crashes on reduced-fidelity sensor input), Teleoperation's adaptive strategy fails and availability is lost. Mitigation: Create explicit Payload Degradation Matrix; require Vehicle module sign-off on all degradation modes; conduct compatibility testing before deployment.

RISK R4 (PREDICTIVE MODEL STALENESS): If Link Quality Prediction Service model is not updated regularly or validated on holdout test sets, model accuracy degrades over time and failover effectiveness decreases. Mitigation: Formalize model training/validation/deployment SLA; implement automated model retraining pipeline; define fallback strategy (e.g., revert to reactive failover) if model is unavailable.

RISK R5 (MONITORING LATENCY CHAIN): If monitoring latency (collection + transmission + processing) exceeds 50ms, combined with failover decision time (100ms) and switch time (100ms), total latency (250ms+) may exceed link degradation duration, causing availability loss. Mitigation: Formalize end-to-end monitoring latency SLA; conduct latency profiling across all monitoring pathways; implement low-latency instrumentation (e.g., hardware timestamping, dedicated monitoring channels).

RISK R6 (AVAILABILITY MEASUREMENT AMBIGUITY): If 'downtime' definition is not formalized (e.g., what link quality threshold triggers downtime, how long must link be degraded to count as downtime), availability measurement will be subjective and non-reproducible. Mitigation: Formalize availability measurement protocol with explicit thresholds, calculation method, and instrumentation requirements; conduct measurement validation on historical data.

RISK R7 (DEGRADATION PRIORITY CONFLICTS): If priority order for adaptive degradation is not formalized (e.g., should operator commands be degraded before vehicle telemetry), Teleoperation may make suboptimal degradation decisions that violate safety constraints. Mitigation: Formalize Graceful Degradation Priority Matrix with Safety Services approval; implement priority enforcement in Teleoperation logic; audit degradation decisions against priority matrix.

RISK R8 (INTERFACE DIFFUSION): If interface contracts (Network Link SLA, Safety Services escalation, Vehicle Payload Degradation) are not formalized, ownership boundaries will blur during implementation, leading to duplicated logic, inconsistent behavior, and difficult-to-trace failures. Mitigation: Formalize all interface contracts as explicit design specifications; assign ownership and sign-off authority for each interface; implement interface versioning and change control.
evidence_needed: EVIDENCE E1: Network Link module specification confirming failover command execution latency ≤100ms and link status reporting frequency ≥10Hz. (Needed before DP6 finalization.)
EVIDENCE E2: Safety Services module specification defining safety-critical message list, escalation protocol, and veto authority over degradation policies. (Needed before DP6 finalization.)
EVIDENCE E3: Vehicle module specification confirming graceful handling of all degradation modes in Payload Degradation Matrix. (Needed before DP6 finalization.)
EVIDENCE E4: Link Quality Prediction Service specification (if ML-based) defining model training/validation/deployment SLA, accuracy targets, and fallback strategy. (Needed before DP6 finalization.)

EVIDENCE E5: Instrumentation and observability specification confirming Teleoperation can log all failover events, link quality samples, degradation events, and safety-critical message delivery with ≤50ms latency. (Needed before DP6 finalization.)
EVIDENCE E6: Availability measurement protocol specification defining measurement window, calculation method, downtime definition, and audit trail requirements. (Needed before DP6 finalization.)
EVIDENCE E7: Graceful Degradation Priority Matrix formalized and approved by Safety Services. (Needed before DP6 finalization.)

EVIDENCE E8: Latency stress test results confirming end-to-end monitoring latency ≤50ms under worst-case conditions (high CPU load, network congestion, failover events). (Needed before implementation.)
EVIDENCE E9: Payload degradation compatibility test results confirming Vehicle module handles all degradation modes without crashes or safety violations. (Needed before implementation.)
EVIDENCE E10: Model validation results confirming Link Quality Prediction Service achieves ≥95% precision for link degradation detection on holdout test set. (Needed before deployment.)
